cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: Stop buying AI and start buying capabilities
stories.plain: A four-part framework strips the "AI-powered" label from vendor pitches and forces buyers to ask what a product actually does. This matters because martech procurement decisions are increasingly made on buzzwords alone; a structured evaluation method helps teams compare real capabilities rather than marketing claims.

stories.head: What CMOs should be doing about AI right now
stories.plain: Practical guidance for marketing leaders on building AI literacy across teams, restructuring workflows, and governing AI-driven change before it outpaces the organisation. Relevant to anyone helping a marketing department decide where and how to adopt AI tools responsibly.

stories.head: Your best practices may already be outdated
stories.plain: AI is quietly invalidating long-held marketing assumptions — from audience segmentation to content cadence. The piece challenges practitioners to audit inherited playbooks against AI-reshaped buyer behaviour, making it a useful prompt for teams still running pre-2024 strategies.

stories.head: Microsoft and Mistral expand partnership for enterprise AI in regulated industries
stories.plain: Microsoft commits compute to Mistral's European expansion and will offer Mistral models — including fully disconnected deployment options — across its AI platform. For regulated-industry marketers, this opens a path to frontier generative AI without sending data to US clouds, directly affecting how AI is bought and governed.

stories.head: OpenAI captures 24.6% of AI citations; CTM adds OpenAI Ads attribution
stories.plain: A 5W study finds OpenAI answers now account for nearly a quarter of all AI-generated citations, raising the stakes for generative engine optimisation. The parallel news that CallTrackingMetrics has added OpenAI Ads attribution signals that measuring AI-driven traffic is becoming a standard martech requirement.

stories.head: Stop buying AI and start buying capabilities
stories.plain: The article argues that "AI-powered" is a meaningless label that obscures what a product actually does, and offers a four-part framework for evaluating vendor claims on concrete capabilities instead. This directly changes how marketing teams should approach procurement decisions — a practical tool for buyers navigating an oversaturated market.

stories.head: Agents move quickly, but your accountability is permanent
stories.plain: Marketing agents are now executing decisions on budgets, targeting, and messaging before any human reviews them. The piece argues that the real ceiling on agentic AI adoption is not model capability but the organization's ability to log actions and set hard boundaries — a governance argument every marketing leader needs to hear.

stories.head: Salesforce's Agentforce stumbles reveal marketing's agentic AI readiness gap
stories.plain: Slow adoption of Salesforce Agentforce is being traced to poor data quality and operational unreadiness, not feature gaps. This is a concrete, high-profile example showing why enterprises can't just buy agentic AI — they have to fix the data and process foundations first, which reshapes how AI is built and governed.

stories.head: Agent visibility scores are model-dependent, not fixed metrics
stories.plain: A brand's ability to be found and recommended by AI agents varies significantly depending on which model the agent runs and when. This challenges the assumption that GEO scores are stable, meaning marketers must test visibility across multiple models continuously — a structural change to how brand discoverability is measured.

stories.head: Why AI-ready data infrastructure is so hard to build
stories.plain: Many companies stall trying to perfect all their data before deploying AI, but research suggests starting with one high-value problem works better. The article reframes data infrastructure as an iterative AI prerequisite rather than a one-time project — practical guidance that shapes how marketing AI gets built and prioritized.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Agentic AI is outpacing accountability
body: AI agents are being deployed faster than the governance frameworks needed to manage them. Salesforce's public stumbles signal that even mature enterprise platforms are struggling to make agentic systems predictable and auditable at scale.

The warning is clear: speed of deployment is not the same as readiness. Marketers who move agents into production workflows without accountability structures — who approves an action, who owns an error — are taking on liability that no vendor will absorb for them.
ev: Agile Brand · agent visibility is model-dependent

themes.head: Buy capabilities, not AI labels
body: Vendors are slapping 'AI' on every feature, making it nearly impossible for buyers to compare real value. The emerging discipline is to evaluate what a tool actually enables a team to do — specific, measurable capabilities — rather than the model or marketing claim behind it.

This reframe changes the procurement conversation entirely. CMOs are being urged to retire best-practice playbooks that were written before generative AI existed and to challenge every existing contract on outcome metrics, not feature checklists.
ev: MarTech · what CMOs should do now

themes.head: The data infrastructure gap blocks enterprise AI
body: AI adoption in marketing keeps stalling not because the models are inadequate but because the underlying data is fragmented, siloed, or ungoverned. Building AI-ready infrastructure requires unified workflows and clean data pipelines — work most enterprises haven't finished.

Vendors are responding: Creatio is targeting the 'Frankenstack' of disconnected tools, and Microsoft-Mistral is pitching controllable frontier AI for regulated industries. But integration debt is real, and no platform acquisition resolves it overnight.

themes.head: Agentic commerce redraws the buyer journey
body: AI agents are beginning to shop, research, and negotiate on behalf of consumers, bypassing traditional search and discovery channels. This means brand visibility is becoming model-dependent — if an agent's underlying model doesn't surface your product, you simply don't exist in that purchase path.

B2B buying is equally affected. TrustRadius data shows a widening disconnect between how vendors market and how buyers actually evaluate; trust signals and peer validation now matter more than ever when the first 'reader' of your content may be an AI agent filtering options.

themes.head: AI democratises audience intelligence & measurement
body: Capabilities that once required enterprise data teams — omnichannel audience modelling, CTV incrementality testing, intent-based targeting — are being packaged into accessible, often affordable tools. The Nexxen-Acxiom expansion and sub-$10 AI CRMs for small businesses are two ends of the same trend: precision marketing is no longer gated by budget alone.

Yet access doesn't guarantee use. Marketers self-report knowing what improves paid media ROI but consistently underinvest in measurement infrastructure. The gap is now a strategic choice, not a capability constraint, and that shifts the blame — and the opportunity — squarely onto marketing leadership.

month.takeaway: Across every layer of the stack, the decisive question has shifted from 'do you have AI?' to whether your governance, data, and measurement foundations can actually support it.
